text: Skeletal editing of heteroarenes by a catalyst-free atom-pair swap reaction Precise control over the core structure of heteroarenes is generally difficult to achieve because of the high energy barrier required for the dearomatization process and the strong aromaticity of the cyclic ring. Although some skeletal editing methods, such as the single atom insertion or deletion of certain arenes, have been developed, the direct atom-pair swap reactions are rare, and especially, the straightforward editing of pyridines, one of the most frequent cores found in drugs and materials, remains elusive. Here, we report a general and modular skeletal editing approach allowing for the straightforward conversion of pyridines into various substituted benzenes and naphthalenes by means of a catalyst-free CN to CC atom-pair swap reaction. Our method relies on a one-pot approach involving the dearomative cyclization to form stable oxazino pyridine moiety, a [4+2] cycloaddition with a large variety of dienophiles and, finally, a rearomatization retrocycloaddition. High regioselectivity and good yields of the corresponding arenes are obtained over a wide range of functionalized substrates including drugs, which shows the full compatibility of our approach with complex structures. We finally explore the versatility of this methodology for the late stage modification of drugs as well as for fragment coupling reactions for the assembly of drug-like molecules. The computationally supported mechanism underlying this approach points towards an asynchronicity during the cycloaddition step controlling the regioselectivity of the editing reaction, with major importance assigned to the C-C bond connecting the heteroarene ring with the oxazino scaffold of the adduct. The developed modular skeletal editing approach affords an efficient route to generate efficiently complex multi-substituted arenes with optimized regiochemistry that is not easily achieved by functionalization of their peripheral sites.
